seo.title: Seedance 2.0 | AI Video Model, Pricing, Native Audio & Use Cases | MaxVideoAI
seo.description: Use Seedance 2.0 on MaxVideoAI for multi-shot video, native audio, current pricing, key capabilities, and the best use cases for current Seedance workflows.

overview: Seedance 2.0 is the current Seedance AI video model on MaxVideoAI for multi-shot video, native audio, and reference-guided workflows when you need the main Seedance path for polished ads, launches, and cinematic branded sequences.
pricingNotes: Seedance 2 uses Fal token pricing. MaxVideoAI adds its margin and rounds up to the next cent; Generate shows the final quote before submission.

hero.title: Seedance 2.0 — current Seedance model for multi-shot video and native audio
hero.intro: Seedance 2.0 is the current Seedance AI video model for multi-shot video, native audio, and reference-guided workflows when you need the main Seedance path for polished ads, launches, and cinematic branded sequences. Use this page to review pricing, key capabilities, best use cases, and when this Seedance video workflow is a better fit than Fast or older Seedance versions.
hero.badge: 3 workflows · 480p/720p · Auto or 4-15s · Native audio · 9 image / 3 video / 3 audio refs

items.title: Action scenes with collisions, debris, and more believable physical interaction
Multi-shot ads with consistent product and scene continuity
Audio-led scenes with synced dialogue, ambience, and sound effects
Storyboard-to-video using text and multimodal references
Director-style camera moves, transitions, and framing control
Pre-visualization before final edit (concept to shot list to rough cut)

technicalOverview.label: Role
technicalOverview.body: ByteDance's latest Seedance AI video model with a unified multimodal audio-video architecture.

technicalOverview.label: Inputs
technicalOverview.body: Three workflow types in one family: text-to-video, start image + optional end image in i2v, plus image/video/audio references in ref2v

technicalOverview.label: Reference limits
technicalOverview.body: Up to 9 images, 3 video clips, and 3 audio clips, with 12 total files across all ref2v inputs

technicalOverview.label: Audio
technicalOverview.body: Native audio is on by default and can be disabled; Fal states the price is the same either way

technicalOverview.label: Camera and motion
technicalOverview.body: Advanced camera control with strong results on dolly zooms, rack focus, tracking shots, POV switches, and smooth handheld movement

technicalOverview.label: Notes
technicalOverview.body: Fal's public page highlights text-to-video, image-to-video, reference-to-video, and optimized fast variants inside the Seedance 2 family.

promptStructure.title: Prompting Seedance 2.0
promptStructure.quote: [Genre] + [subject] + [setting] + [action beats] + [camera language] + [audio cues] + [duration auto or 4-15s] + [shot structure].
promptStructure.description: For best consistency, keep each beat explicit and define camera transitions and audio timing in the same prompt.
steps: Add dialogue, ambience, and SFX cues

promptingTip: Tip: start with Subject -> Action -> Camera -> Style, then choose the right Seedance path: prompt-only text-to-video, start/end-frame image-to-video, or multimodal reference-to-video.

tips.title: Production notes
items: Keep prompts structured shot-by-shot when you want the strongest multi-shot continuity.
Use multimodal references to lock wardrobe, props, pacing, and ambience across beats.
Use the optional end image in i2v when you need a guided last frame.
Move to Seedance 2.0 Fast when you need cheaper draft passes before final renders.
Generate shows the active route and the final quote before you submit.

prompts.title: Action chase (multi-shot)
prompts.prompt: Three-shot rooftop chase at dusk, shot 1 wide tracking run, shot 2 handheld close pursuit, shot 3 crane reveal over skyline, realistic footstep impacts and city sirens, 15 seconds total.

prompts.title: Continuous spy thriller
prompts.prompt: A spy exits a neon train station, camera follows over shoulder into a rainy alley, rack focus to a coded message, low synth score and distant PA announcements, 12 seconds.

prompts.title: 3-shot commercial
prompts.prompt: Shot 1 macro of watch crown with rim light, shot 2 wrist hero turntable in studio haze, shot 3 lifestyle close-up in city night rain, punchy transitions and polished ad soundtrack, 15 seconds.

prompts.title: Multimodal storyboard reference
prompts.prompt: Use @Image1..@Image9 as storyboard style anchors, @Video1..@Video3 for pacing references, @Audio1..@Audio3 for rhythm and ambience, keep wardrobe continuity and cinematic 16:9 framing.

prompts.title: Audio-forward dialogue scene
prompts.prompt: Two-character dialogue in a subway platform at night, synchronized dialogue, ambience and metallic train brakes on cue, smooth dolly and cut-in close-up, stereo mix feel, 10 seconds.

faqs.a: Seedance 2.0 is best for polished Seedance AI video workflows, multi-shot ads, branded sequences, audio-led scenes, and reference-guided production where continuity matters more than draft speed.
Yes. Seedance 2.0 supports native audio, including dialogue, ambience, music, and sound effects inside the same generation workflow.

faqs.q: How is Seedance 2.0 different from Seedance 2.0 Fast?
faqs.a: Seedance 2.0 is the stronger current choice for final-quality multi-shot work, native audio, and more demanding reference-driven outputs, while Seedance 2.0 Fast is better for cheaper draft passes and quicker route comparisons.

faqs.q: When should I use Seedance 2.0 instead of Seedance 1.5 Pro?
faqs.a: Use Seedance 2.0 when you need stronger multi-shot continuity, broader reference workflows, and the current Seedance production path. Seedance 1.5 Pro still fits shorter, simpler older workflows.

faqs.q: Is Seedance 2.0 good for multi-shot video?
faqs.a: Yes. Seedance 2.0 is specifically well suited to multi-shot outputs, guided transitions, and maintaining scene continuity across short cinematic sequences.

specSections.title: Multimodal input stack
specSections.intro: Fal's public page frames Seedance 2.0 as a unified multimodal audio-video model.
items: Up to 12 total files across the ref2v run

specSections.title: Output style and structure
specSections.intro: Fal highlights cinematic control, native audio, realistic physics, multi-shot cuts, and up to 15 seconds per generation.
items: Image-to-video supports an optional end frame
Audio toggle available with the same pricing whether audio is on or off

custom.promptingIntro: Seedance 2.0 works best when the brief is ordered and the workflow is explicit. Start with Subject -> Action -> Camera -> Style, then decide whether the shot should stay prompt-led, use a start/end image pair, or lean on multimodal references.
promptingGlobalPrinciples: Write the core brief in Subject -> Action -> Camera -> Style order.
Keep one readable action per shot and add timestamps when you want internal cuts.
Give every reference one job: images for identity/style, videos for pacing/camera, audio for rhythm/ambience.
Use the optional end image to define the landing frame, not a second unrelated scene.
Keep dialogue short and pin SFX to visible actions.
promptingEngineWhy: Text-to-video is strong for fresh ideation, cinematic camera language, and multi-shot beat planning.
Image-to-video is strongest when the start frame stays fixed and the optional end frame defines the landing pose or composition.
Reference-to-video is where Seedance gains its biggest continuity advantage across characters, props, pacing, and audio mood.
Native audio stays cleaner when music, ambience, dialogue, and SFX are short and tied to visible beats.

promptingTabNotes.quick: Text = prompt-only generation from a clean brief.
promptingTabNotes.structured: Start/End = guide the opening frame and the landing frame.
promptingTabNotes.pro: References = assign each image, video, and audio file a clear job.
promptingTabNotes.storyboard: Timeline = use timestamps when you want internal cuts or multi-shot structure.

promptingTabs.label: Text
promptingTabs.title: Text-to-video prompt
promptingTabs.description: Use this when the shot starts from language, not from uploaded assets.
promptingTabs.copy:
Subject:
[Who/what appears + 2-3 defining traits]

Action:
[One visible action or one timed beat]

Camera:
[Shot size + angle + one move + optional transition verb]

Style:
[Lighting + palette + texture / lens feel]

Audio:
[Ambience + 1-2 SFX cues + optional short dialogue]

Example:
A courier in a soaked yellow jacket sprints through a narrow alley at night. He jumps a puddle and looks back once. Camera: wide tracking shot into a short handheld close-up. Style: wet asphalt reflections, cold blue street light, subtle film grain. Audio: footsteps, distant siren, one short breathy line.

promptingTabs.label: Start/End
promptingTabs.title: Image-to-video with optional end frame
promptingTabs.description: Use this when the first frame is fixed and you want to guide the landing frame.
promptingTabs.copy:
Start image:
[What must stay fixed from frame 1: subject, wardrobe, props, location]

End image (optional):
[What the last frame should land on: pose, composition, camera distance]

Motion path:
[How the subject moves between the two frames]

Camera:
[One move only: push-in / arc / handheld / static]

Style + lighting:
[What must stay consistent]

Audio:
[Ambience / SFX / short dialogue if relevant]

Rule:
Do not ask the end frame to become a different scene. Use it to control the landing frame, not to restart the story.

promptingTabs.label: References
promptingTabs.title: Reference-to-video prompt
promptingTabs.description: Use this when identity, pacing, or ambience must stay locked.

promptingTabs.label: Timeline
promptingTabs.title: Timeline / multi-shot prompt
promptingTabs.description: Use this when one generation contains multiple internal beats or cuts.
promptingTabs.copy:
Duration: [auto / 4-15s]

Shot 1 (0-5s):
[Framing + action + camera move]

Transition:
[match cut / whip pan / crash zoom / settle]

Shot 2 (5-10s):
[Framing + action + camera move]

Shot 3 (10-15s):
[Reveal / reaction / product payoff / final pose]

Continuity:
[Wardrobe + props + location + lighting]

Audio plan:
[Ambience bed + 1-3 timed SFX + optional short dialogue]

Use auto duration only when the model should infer the pacing for you.

strengths: Strong cinematic camera direction when beats are explicit.
Native audio sync in short multi-shot outputs.
High utility for ads, action beats, and storyboard prototyping.
troubleshootingItems: Cuts feel abrupt -> add transition verbs and timestamps (match cut at 5s, whip pan into Shot 2).
Continuity drifts -> add anchors (wardrobe, prop, location) and reuse references.
Audio mismatch -> shorten dialogue, pin SFX to visible action, keep one ambience bed.
Physics looks off -> simplify simultaneous actions per beat and reduce fast interactions.
boundaries: Complex action stacks still benefit from simpler, clearly separated beats.
Reference-heavy prompts work best when each source has one clear job.
Reference audio only works when at least one image or video reference is attached.
Check the active Fal route family before scaling production traffic.
Use the final quote in Generate before client-facing rollout or automated workflows.
